content.text: Template version upgrade path is the system by which projects generated from a starter template can be brought forward to newer template versions after initial generation. Unlike library dependencies — where an upgrade changes code that the project imports — template upgrades must reconcile changes in the template source with customizations the project team has made to their generated files since generation. The upgrade path defines how conflicts between template changes and team customizations are detected, resolved, and validated.

A complete upgrade path system includes: semantic versioning for templates, an answers file that records the variable values used at generation time, a tool that performs three-way merges between the old template version, the new template version, and the project's current state, migration scripts that handle breaking schema changes between major versions, and validation steps that confirm the upgraded project still passes CI gates and eval suites before the upgrade is committed.

Templates that cannot be upgraded are templates with a one-way door: once a project is generated and the team begins customizing it, the team is permanently cut off from improvements to the template. Security patches, new observability tooling, improved error handling patterns, and upgraded dependency configurations that the platform team adds to the template will never reach projects generated from earlier versions. Over time, the fleet of generated projects diverges from current best practices and creates an accumulating upgrade debt that becomes prohibitively expensive to address manually.

A well-designed upgrade path turns template maintenance from a platform concern into a team workflow: platform teams release new template versions with changelogs, teams choose when to upgrade, the upgrade tool does the mechanical work of merging changes, and teams review and test the result. This pattern keeps the entire fleet of generated projects closer to current platform standards without requiring manual work by the platform team for each generated project.
Security and dependency patches in the template propagate to all projects that upgrade, compressing the window of exposure.
Teams can defer upgrades until a convenient time rather than being forced onto new versions immediately.
Three-way merge tooling makes the upgrade diff reviewable, so teams understand exactly what the template change is doing to their project.
Migration scripts automate the mechanical parts of breaking schema changes, reducing upgrade friction.
The upgrade history is recorded in version control, making it possible to audit when each project was last upgraded and to which version.
Platform teams can measure upgrade adoption rates and identify projects that are significantly behind.
Teams that heavily customize generated files accumulate merge conflicts on every upgrade, eventually making the upgrade path more expensive than manual updates.
Maintaining migration scripts for breaking changes is additional authoring work for the platform team.
Semantic versioning of templates requires disciplined classification of changes as patch, minor, or major.
Teams must commit and maintain an answers file to enable reproducible upgrades; projects without this file cannot use the upgrade tooling.
Upgrade validation requires a working CI environment for the generated project, which not all teams have configured immediately after generation.

The upgrade tool (Copier, Renovate for templates, or a custom CLI) reads the pinned template version and the answers file from the project being upgraded. It fetches the new template version, computes the diff between old template and new template, and applies that diff to the project using a three-way merge: base = old template rendered with answers, ours = project's current state, theirs = new template rendered with answers. Files where the team has not made customizations upgrade cleanly; files where the team has customized content produce merge conflicts for the team to resolve.

Migration scripts handle changes that cannot be expressed as file-content merges: variable renames, removed variables, new required variables without defaults, and restructured configuration files. A migration script for a major version upgrade reads the old answers file, transforms it to the new schema, and writes the updated answers file before the merge step runs. This allows the merge to proceed with the correct variable context.

After the merge and any migration scripts run, the upgrade tool stages the changes as a branch or patch for team review. Teams run their CI pipeline against the upgraded branch; if tests pass and evals pass, they merge the upgrade PR. The answers file is updated with the new template version pin so that subsequent upgrades start from the correct baseline.

Invest in the upgrade path infrastructure when the template fleet reaches five or more generated projects and the platform team begins releasing template updates that include improvements teams would benefit from. Earlier than that, manual upgrades are cheap enough. Later than that, the absence of upgrade tooling creates significant technical debt as teams begin diverging from the template in ways that block future upgrades.

Prioritize the upgrade path for templates in regulated domains — security tooling, compliance infrastructure, audit logging — where keeping projects current with platform standards has compliance implications. For templates in lower-stakes domains, a documented manual upgrade process may be sufficient until the fleet grows large enough to justify automated tooling.

# .copier-answers.yml — committed to every generated project root
# Records the variable values used at generation time
_commit: v1.2.0   # template version pin
_src_path: git+https://github.com/internal/agent-templates.git

service_name: alert-router
team_name: platform-sre
model_provider: anthropic
addons:
  - slack-notifier
feature_flags:
  enable_tracing: true
  enable_cost_alerts: true
  enable_eval_suite: false
cost_center: CC-1042

# Upgrade a project to the latest template version
# Copier reads .copier-answers.yml for the current pin

# Dry-run: show what would change without applying
copier update --dry-run .

# Apply upgrade interactively (prompts for conflict resolution)
copier update .

# Apply upgrade non-interactively (stages conflicts for later resolution)
copier update --defaults .

# After resolving conflicts, validate
npm test && npm run eval:smoke

# migration_scripts/v1_to_v2.py — run by upgrade tool before merge
# Handles breaking schema changes in major version upgrade
import sys, yaml
from pathlib import Path

answers_path = Path(sys.argv[1]) if len(sys.argv) > 1 else Path('.copier-answers.yml')

with open(answers_path) as f:
    answers = yaml.safe_load(f)

# v2 renamed 'model_provider' to 'llm_provider'
if 'model_provider' in answers:
    answers['llm_provider'] = answers.pop('model_provider')

# v2 requires 'environment' variable (new required field with default)
if 'environment' not in answers:
    answers['environment'] = 'production'  # safe default

# v2 moved feature_flags.enable_cost_alerts to top-level cost_alerts_enabled
flags = answers.get('feature_flags', {})
if 'enable_cost_alerts' in flags:
    answers['cost_alerts_enabled'] = flags.pop('enable_cost_alerts')

answers['_commit'] = 'v2.0.0'

with open(answers_path, 'w') as f:
    yaml.dump(answers, f, default_flow_style=False, sort_keys=False)

print(f"Migrated {answers_path} from v1 schema to v2 schema")

A team running project-alpha, generated from template v1.0.0, wants to pick up the new distributed tracing integration added in template v1.2.0. They run `copier update --dry-run` to preview the changes: three new files are added (tracing configuration, middleware, and test fixtures), and `requirements.txt` has three new entries. The team has not customized any of these files, so the update applies cleanly. They run their CI pipeline against the updated branch, it passes, and they merge the upgrade PR. Their `.copier-answers.yml` is updated to pin v1.2.0.

A second team running project-gamma is upgrading from v1.0.0 to v2.0.0 (a major version with breaking changes). The migration script runs first, renaming the `model_provider` variable to `llm_provider` in their answers file. The merge then runs: two files have conflicts because the team customized them after generation. The team resolves the conflicts manually, choosing to keep their custom retry logic while accepting the template's updated error handling for the remainder of the function. They update their answers file to pin v2.0.0.

The platform team uses a Renovate-like scanning job that runs weekly against all projects in the internal catalog. The scanner reads each project's `.copier-answers.yml`, compares the pinned version to the current latest template version, and creates GitHub issues for projects that are more than one minor version behind. Projects more than one major version behind get escalated to the owning team's tech lead. This proactive scanning keeps the fleet from accumulating large version gaps that make upgrades expensive.

The Copier project — widely used in the Python ecosystem and adopted by several large internal developer platforms — implements exactly this three-way merge upgrade model. Projects generated with Copier commit a `.copier-answers.yml` to their repository. When the platform team releases a new template version, developers run `copier update`, which fetches the new template, applies the three-way merge, and prompts for conflict resolution on customized files. Copier's changelog documents which template changes are patch (bug fixes, non-breaking additions), minor (new optional variables, new optional files), and major (schema changes, removed variables, restructured output), so teams can estimate upgrade complexity before running the command.
Not committing the answers file to version control, making upgrades impossible without re-entering all variable values.
Treating the generated project as a template source rather than a generated output, making heavy customizations that block future upgrades.
Releasing breaking schema changes in minor versions, surprising teams who upgrade expecting non-breaking changes.
Writing migration scripts that are not idempotent — running the migration twice should produce the same result, not corrupt the answers file.
Forgetting to update the pinned template version in answers.yaml after an upgrade, causing the next upgrade to start from the wrong baseline.
Not running CI against the upgraded branch before merging, accepting upgrades that introduce regressions.
Adopt semantic versioning with a clear contract: patch = bug fixes only, minor = backward-compatible additions, major = breaking schema changes.
Write a changelog entry for every template release that describes what changed and which types of projects are most affected.
Test migration scripts against a set of representative answers files before releasing a major version.
Automate upgrade scanning: a weekly job that reports which projects are behind and by how much is more effective than relying on teams to track template releases.
Encourage teams to minimize post-generation customization by building more of what they need into the template variable system and hooks.
Provide an upgrade guide document for each major version that explains the breaking changes and how the migration script handles them.

The computational cost of a single upgrade is low — fetching a template version, running a three-way merge, and executing migration scripts typically completes in under a minute. The scalability challenge is organizational: as the fleet grows to hundreds of generated projects, the platform team must have visibility into which projects are on which template version and which have pending conflicts from a previous failed upgrade attempt. A template version registry or dashboard that aggregates this data is more useful than any performance optimization.

For large fleets, batch upgrade tooling that opens upgrade PRs automatically for all projects on an outdated template version can accelerate adoption of critical security patches. The tool opens a PR per project, runs CI, and notifies the owning team. Teams review and merge on their own schedule. Projects where CI fails on the auto-upgrade PR require manual intervention, typically because of merge conflicts in customized files. Tracking auto-upgrade PR merge rates gives the platform team signal on which template changes are causing the most friction.
Low for clean, high for conflicted
Behavior shared via dependency, not template

The upgrade path itself is a security-relevant operation because it introduces template-supplied code changes into projects. Upgrade PRs must go through the same code review process as other changes: a team member must review the diff, not just merge the automated PR without inspection. For templates that include hook scripts, upgrading the template can change which hook scripts run in the project's CI pipeline, potentially introducing or removing security checks. Code review of upgrade PRs should explicitly verify that hook script changes are expected and safe.

Answers files committed to repositories may contain sensitive configuration values — cost center codes, team identifiers, feature flag settings. These files are typically not sensitive enough to require encryption, but they should not contain secrets, API keys, or credentials. If variable values reference secrets (a vault path, a secret name), only the reference should be in the answers file, not the secret value itself. This keeps the answers file safely committable while keeping actual secrets in the vault.

Implementing these practices effectively requires understanding both the technical and organizational context in which they will operate. Teams at different stages of AI maturity will apply these practices differently: a team that has operated AI systems in production for two years has a richer base of operational experience to inform their implementation choices than a team deploying their first agent. Neither team should simply adopt a template; both should evaluate each practice against their specific risk profile, operational constraints, and team capabilities.

Cross-functional collaboration is essential for sustainable implementation. Engineering, AI safety, product, and SRE teams all have legitimate interests in how these practices are designed and enforced. Practices designed without input from the teams who must live with them tend to be either too abstract to apply consistently or too prescriptive to accommodate the real variability in situations they will encounter. Invest in the collaborative design process upfront; the time spent building shared understanding saves time in adoption and reduces the resistance that comes from practices imposed without consultation.

Measurement is the foundation of continuous improvement. Each practice described here has an associated metric: review cycle time, DoD completion rate, eval pass rate, incident rate, checklist failure distribution. Teams that track these metrics and review them regularly can identify whether their implementations are achieving their intended effects, where friction is causing workarounds, and where investment in tooling or capacity would provide the highest return. Practices that are not measured are not improved; they either remain static while the environment changes around them or gradually decay as their original purpose is forgotten.

Finally, recognize that the practices described here are means, not ends. The ends are: agent capabilities that work reliably, behave safely, can be operated and maintained by the team, and create genuine value for users. Every practice should be evaluated against these outcomes. When a practice is creating overhead without demonstrably contributing to these outcomes, it should be reformed or replaced. When a practice is clearly preventing incidents or improving quality, it should be maintained even when it creates friction. The discipline of outcome-oriented engineering process management is what separates teams that maintain effective practices long-term from teams whose practices gradually become compliance exercises.

Template upgrade governance requires alignment between platform teams, who author and release template versions, and product teams, who consume generated projects and must schedule upgrade work. Without explicit governance, upgrade decisions are ad-hoc: some teams upgrade eagerly and report issues, others never upgrade and accumulate risk. A governance model assigns responsibilities: platform teams are responsible for releasing well-tested versions with migration scripts and changelogs; product teams are responsible for upgrading within a defined window (typically 90 days for minor versions, 180 days for major versions with breaking changes).

SLA-driven upgrade policies work well in organizations where the template fleet is large enough that informal coordination is insufficient. Under such a policy, each project's pinned template version is tracked in the service catalog alongside the project's risk tier. High-risk services (those handling payment data, user PII, or safety-critical decisions) are subject to stricter upgrade SLAs — they must be on a current minor version at all times. Lower-risk services have more flexibility. The platform team reports compliance with these SLAs quarterly, and teams that are chronically behind receive escalation from engineering leadership.

Incentive alignment is subtle but important: teams that invest in keeping their generated projects close to the current template version experience lower upgrade friction over time because each individual upgrade is smaller. Teams that defer upgrades accumulate a larger delta that makes the eventual upgrade more complex and more likely to have conflicts. Communicating this long-term cost dynamic clearly — ideally with data from the team's own upgrade history — helps teams make informed decisions about deferral.

For templates in domains where regulatory compliance matters, the upgrade path documentation should reference the compliance controls that template updates maintain. When a template update rotates TLS certificate pinning, adds a required audit log field, or updates a dependency with a known CVE, the changelog should note the compliance implication. This gives product teams the business justification needed to prioritize upgrade work against competing feature work in sprint planning.

A healthy upgrade program is one where the average version lag across the fleet is low and trending downward or stable, auto-upgrade PRs have a high success rate (CI passes on the first attempt), and the time between template release and fleet-wide adoption is shrinking. Measure these metrics from data in the service catalog and CI system: version lag per project, auto-upgrade PR failure rate by template version, and median days-to-upgrade for each release. Report these metrics to engineering leadership alongside the template release cadence.

When metrics indicate that a particular template release caused a spike in upgrade failures or extended the average days-to-upgrade, investigate the release for common causes: a migration script that produced incorrect output, a merge conflict in a file that most teams have customized, or a new required variable without a sensible default. Post-mortems on difficult upgrades feed directly into improvements to the template authoring and release process, creating a feedback loop that reduces upgrade friction over time.
An upgrade path system enables generated projects to benefit from future template improvements rather than diverging permanently after generation.
Commit an answers file with every generated project: it records variable values and template version pin, enabling reproducible upgrades.
Three-way merge tools handle the mechanical work; teams focus on resolving conflicts in files they have customized.
Migration scripts automate breaking schema changes between major versions, reducing the manual effort of major version upgrades.
Proactive upgrade scanning keeps the fleet from accumulating large version gaps that make upgrades prohibitively expensive.
Upgrade PRs require code review — template changes introduce code into projects and should not be merged without human inspection.
